content.full_text:
A-91-122(Caves)

This letter is SUPERSEDED by the 1998 amendments to

Regulation 18530. Regulation 18530 revised the Commission's

interpretation of Government Code Section 85300. Prior to the

amendment, the Commission interpreted Section 85300 to prohibit the

use of public resources for campaign purposes. Amended Regulation

18530 provides that Section 85300 only prohibits the payment of public

moneys for the public financing of elections.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 2, 1991        SUPERSEDED BY 1998
AMENDMENTS TO
REGULATION 18530


Karin Caves
2440 California Avenue
Carmichael, CA 95608

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-122

Dear Ms. Caves:

     This is in response to your request for advice with respect
to the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/  This
letter confirms the advice provided to you by Scott Hallabrin of
this office.

     As Mr. Hallabrin explained, the Commission's advice is
limited to the provisions of the Act.  Since your question may
implicate other areas of the law, we suggest that you consult the
Attorney General's office, the Office of the Legislative Counsel
and your own agency's list of incompatible activities concerning
this question.

     You stated that you are currently employed as the chief
consultant to the Assembly Labor and Employment Committee in the
office of Assemblymember Terry Friedman.  You have been offered a
contract to provide consulting services for a California member of
Congress who is beginning exploratory activity in connection with
election to the United States Senate.  You will act as the
candidate's spokesperson and advise the candidate regarding media
relations.

     You are considering reducing your compensated time at the
Assembly so that you may use your free time to work with the
federal candidate.  However, you have become concerned that in
performing these dual roles you may inadvertently receive
telephone calls at your Assembly office concerning your contract
with the federal candidate and that this might violate the Act.
You have asked whether the Act restricts your ability to work
part-time for both the Assemblymember and the federal candidate.

1/    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-122
Page 2


     The Act does not restrict a public official's ability to
pursue private employment.2/ Section 85300 provides that no
public officer shall expend public moneys for the purpose of
seeking elective office. However, "elective office" is defined in
the Act as any state, regional, county, municipal, district or
judicial office which is filled at an election and would not
include federal offices. (Section 82023.) Moreover, Section
82007 provides that the term "candidate" does not include any
person within the meaning of Section 301(b) of the Federal
Election Campaign Act of 1971.3/ (Section 82007.)

     Consequently, while your question may implicate other areas
of the law, the provisions of the Act do not restrict your
consulting arrangement.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:plh


___________________________

2/  However, receipt of income may result in a conflict of
interest with respect to future decisions in relation to the
source of the income. (See e.g., Sections 87100, et seq.) We
have enclosed a pamphlet on conflicts of interest for your
information.

3/  Under federal law, services to the federal candidate which are
not paid for by the candidate may be contributions. You may wish
to contact the Federal Elections Commission concerning this issue.
